summary: Dan Shapiro argues that equal (50/50) cofounder equity splits are always wrong because they represent avoidance of hard conversations rather than a thoughtful business decision. He presents a formula for calculating equity splits based on factors like who initiated the startup, who brought the idea, prior execution, CEO role, full-time commitment, reputation, and cash investment. He contends that unequal splits force cofounders to confront difficult questions about relative contributions early, which is healthy for the company. He directly rebuts Joel Spolsky's advocacy for equal splits, arguing it confuses 'easy' with 'fair' and doesn't reflect market realities. The core message is that cofounders who can't resolve equity disagreements should discover that incompatibility before taking on investors and employees.
key_points: Founders are defined by working without salary at a stage when the company can't pay anyone — if you draw a salary on day one, you're not a founder
The equity formula accounts for initiation (5%), idea (5%), prior execution (5-25%), CEO role (5%), full-time commitment (200%), reputation (50-500%+), and cash investment
A 50/50 split signals conflict avoidance, not agreement — cofounders need to resolve hard questions before commitments to investors and employees
Reputation and investability can be the single largest factor, with 'super-preneurs' whose involvement guarantees funding commanding outsized equity
Joel Spolsky's equal-split approach confuses 'easy' with 'fair' and over-allocates to salaried employees at the expense of founders who worked without pay
money_quotes: The question of equity brings out the most fundamental differences, perceptions, and values in an aspiring startup. In fact the equity question, more than any other, may strangle a young company before it can even get started. And that's a damn good thing.
The rule is this: if you're working for a company that's so young it can't pay you, you're a founder. If you are drawing a salary on your first day at work, you're not.
It's a lie to say that ideas are worthless and execution is everything… but it's not too far from the truth, either.
If you don't trust your CEO with the majority of shares, you're founding the company with the wrong person.
50/50 isn't a business decision, it's a compromise.
The most common cause of startup death is founders who can't resolve their differences. Nobody hears about it; they just pack up and go home before the company ever had a chance.
If you find that the only way you can get a decision made is by compromising – then you need to stop now, before the price of failure climbs higher.
It confuses 'easy' with 'fair'. If you've had two successful exits already while I'm doing my first startup, it's easy but not fair to split it 50/50.
key_insight: A 50/50 equity split is conflict avoidance masquerading as fairness — cofounders must confront uncomfortable differences in contribution and risk early, because the inability to resolve those questions is itself the strongest signal the partnership will fail.
